overall_assessment.one_sentence_verdict: Baseline fit is good for the actual market behind this category because the curated record already supports architecture-heavy, production-AI, player-coach positioning, but the current master-CV representation under-surfaces AI quality, guardrails, and architect framing.
overall_assessment.uncertainty_note: Category-label noise is material here: the blueprint behaves more like Principal/Lead AI Architect than formal AI executive leadership, and the current representation is proxy-derived rather than a canonical master CV artifact.
citations: BLUEPRINT: senior_ic 33/38, player_coach 12/38, manager 2/38, director 2/38; platform design 36/38; architecture valued evidence 21/21; delivery 20/21; reliability 17/21.

weighted_score_explanation: Coverage is strong because curated evidence already supports platform architecture, modernization, observability, delivery, evaluation, guardrails, mentoring, and stakeholder translation in the current role.
Curation completeness is good but not complete because upstream claimable role-title evidence and likely richer current-role framing remain outside the curated metadata.

Representation quality is the main drag: the current headline and summary surface architecture well but do not yet prioritize production AI platform, evaluation, guardrails, or player-coach framing strongly enough.
AI-architecture fit is the strongest component because the record shows architecture ownership tied to delivery, reliability, and production AI controls rather than research-heavy work.

Leadership-scope fit is solid for player-coach and architect scope, but not for formal executive ownership; evidence supports mentoring, hiring involvement, and stakeholder influence more than org management.

Impact proof is credible because the record includes concrete metrics on incidents, uptime, onboarding, velocity, delivery cadence, users, plugins, and integration scale, though some AI-specific outcome metrics are still more technical than business-facing.
citations: BLUEPRINT: platform design 36/38; architecture 21/21; delivery 20/21; reliability 17/21; evaluation 11/21; governance 10/21; hiring 2/38; budget_pnl 0/38.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Architecture ownership tied to modernization, reliability, and continuous delivery
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: This is the category’s clearest table-stakes signal because the blueprint privileges platform design plus shipped production outcomes over literal executive title strings.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by a legacy-to-event-driven AWS platform transformation with 75% incident reduction, 3 years zero downtime, Architectural Runway refactoring, and an observability pipeline handling billions of events daily.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Production AI platform engineering with evaluation depth
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: The blueprint rewards applied AI integration when it is production-bound and backed by evaluation and quality controls rather than model-research claims.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by Commander-4/Joyia evidence showing 2,000 users, 42 plugins, hybrid retrieval, LLM-as-judge reranking, retrieval evaluation with MRR/NDCG, guardrail profiles, and structured outputs.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Guardrails, structured outputs, and policy-aware rollout
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: Governance is a differentiator in this market when it is framed as practical production control rather than compliance-theater or policy ownership.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by structured output architecture, Zod validation, 5 MCP server tools, per-silo guardrail injection, per-silo access control, and content-policy enforcement.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Reliability and observability as operating discipline
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: The blueprint weights reliability ahead of most named AI methods, so operational proof materially strengthens fit.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by observability architecture, proactive monitoring and alerting, MTTR reduction, blameless postmortems, and an architectural north star built around removing knowledge black holes.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Player-coach leadership through mentoring and technical standards
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: The category values mentoring and technical guidance more than formal org leadership, making this a strong fit signal without title inflation.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by mentoring 10+ senior engineers, promoting 3 engineers to lead positions, introducing DDD standards, and leading a 4-engineer cross-functional SCRUM team earlier in the record.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Stakeholder translation and business-facing architectural influence
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: The blueprint explicitly rewards cross-functional translation and communication because this market is architecture-heavy but stakeholder-exposed.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by educating business stakeholders on technical-debt ROI, consulting as a subject matter expert, aligning core business metrics with technical stacks, and communicating system risks transparently.

gap_analysis.signal: Architect / Technical Architect title-family support is not yet promoted into curated metadata
gap_analysis.why_it_matters: This category should be framed as architect/player-coach, and upstream title evidence would strengthen safe headline and role metadata without inflating to executive scope.
gap_analysis.current_state: The current representation uses a generic headline and the current-role summary references Technical Lead, while claimable upstream STAR records explicitly use 'Lead Software Engineer / Technical Architect' and 'Technical Lead - Addressable TV'.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: Architect/player-coach title-family support appears available upstream, but it is not yet curated into downstream-safe metadata.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Promote the current-role title variants and their provenance into `data/master-cv/role_metadata.json` and the Seven.One role file so architect framing becomes reusable downstream.
gap_analysis.citation: Upstream claimable STAR records support stronger architect framing than the current generic metadata, but they are not yet curated.

gap_analysis.signal: Production AI evaluation and guardrail evidence is curated but underweighted in the master CV
gap_analysis.why_it_matters: Evaluation, reliability, and governance are differentiators in this market; under-surfacing them weakens category fit even when the evidence exists.
gap_analysis.current_state: Commander-4 evaluation, guardrails, and structured-output evidence is curated in role and project files, but the current summary is dominated by legacy modernization and the headline does not surface AI platform quality controls.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: The candidate already has reusable production-AI quality evidence; it needs stronger placement, not new invention.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Surface one Commander-4 evaluation/guardrails achievement in the summary and top-priority Seven.One bullets, ahead of lower-signal stack detail.
gap_analysis.citation: Curated evidence already covers retrieval evaluation, guardrails, structured outputs, and access control, but current representation surfaces them weakly.

gap_analysis.signal: Stakeholder translation and risk communication are curated but underrepresented
gap_analysis.why_it_matters: The blueprint repeatedly values communication and stakeholder alignment, especially for architecture-heavy player-coach roles.
gap_analysis.current_state: Strong curated stakeholder-facing evidence exists in current-role achievements, yet the current summary and priority bullets emphasize system transformation more than translation, risk framing, and business alignment.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: The record supports technical-influence leadership claims without crossing into executive-business ownership.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Pull one stakeholder-translation bullet into the top of the Seven.One narrative and use it to balance architecture with influence.
gap_analysis.citation: Business education on technical debt, SME collaboration, and transparent risk communication are already curated but not sufficiently surfaced.

gap_analysis.signal: Enterprise integration story is fragmented across the representation layer
gap_analysis.why_it_matters: The category values enterprise integration and stakeholder translation, especially where AI or platform capabilities must fit real operating environments.
gap_analysis.current_state: Integration evidence exists across current AI platform work, Samdock API design, and earlier systems-integration roles, but the current representation reads more as architecture plus retrieval tooling than enterprise integration leadership.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: Enterprise integration can be claimed safely when anchored to MCP tools, external integrations, scalable APIs, and legacy-bridging systems already in the record.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Surface one integration-oriented bullet in the summary or top role bullets to connect AI platform work with enterprise-system adoption.
gap_analysis.citation: Curated evidence supports external integrations, API design, and legacy-system bridging, but the master CV does not yet foreground that category-relevant thread.

gap_analysis.signal: Formal executive people-management ownership remains unsafe
gap_analysis.why_it_matters: This category does not require strong org-management evidence, but overstating it would still distort the record and create title-risk.
gap_analysis.current_state: The curated record supports mentoring, promotions, hiring-process leadership, and one 4-engineer team lead example, but it does not establish a sizeable reporting line, budget ownership, or sustained performance-management scope.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: Player-coach, mentoring, technical leadership, and selective hiring claims are safe; formal department leadership is not.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Do not use Head of AI, VP of AI, Engineering Manager, or org-builder framing unless direct reporting-line evidence is added later.
gap_analysis.citation: Current evidence shows coaching and hiring participation, not durable executive-management ownership.

gap_analysis.signal: Custom-model training, fine-tuning, and RLHF claims remain unsafe
gap_analysis.why_it_matters: The blueprint favors applied production AI integration; unsupported frontier-model claims would reduce credibility and are explicitly disallowed by the evidence store.
gap_analysis.current_state: The curated AI record shows retrieval, routing, evaluation, guardrails, semantic caching, and tool-calling architecture, but the do-not-claim ledger explicitly excludes fine-tuning, custom embedding training, and RLHF.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: Applied AI platform and quality-control claims are safe; model-training leadership is not.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Keep AI framing bounded to platform architecture, retrieval quality, evaluation, guardrails, and gateway design.
gap_analysis.citation: The evidence store explicitly blocks frontier-training claims while strongly supporting integration and evaluation work.

gap_analysis.signal: Broad EEA AI governance leadership remains unsafe despite narrow compliance evidence
gap_analysis.why_it_matters: The category label includes EEA, but the blueprint warns against implying region-wide compliance or regulated-industry authority without repeated proof.
gap_analysis.current_state: There is one curated GDPR/TCF compliance achievement plus per-silo content-policy guardrails, but the record does not show broad EEA AI governance ownership across organizations or programs.
gap_analysis.safe_interpretation: Narrow compliance and guardrail implementation claims are safe; region-wide AI governance leadership is not.
gap_analysis.recommended_action: Limit compliance language to documented GDPR/TCF and content-policy controls unless additional directly evidenced governance ownership is curated.
gap_analysis.citation: Existing compliance evidence is real but too narrow to support broad EEA governance-lead claims.

headline_safe: Technical Lead / Software Architect for production AI and event-driven platforms.
Production AI platform architecture with delivery, reliability, evaluation, and guardrails.
Player-coach technical leadership across architecture, stakeholder alignment, and mentoring.
profile_safe: Architected a legacy AdTech platform transformation into event-driven AWS microservices, cutting incidents by 75% and sustaining 3 years of zero downtime.
Platform lead for an enterprise AI workflow platform serving 2,000 users and 42 plugins, with hybrid retrieval, LLM-as-judge reranking, semantic caching, structured outputs, and evaluation harnesses.
Hands-on architect who improves system health and delivery by combining observability, technical-debt strategy, and stakeholder education.
Mentored 10+ senior engineers, promoted 3 to lead positions, and scaled delivery across matrixed cross-functional teams without overstating formal org ownership.
experience_safe: Established architecture north stars, DDD standards, and event-driven design patterns that improved onboarding, velocity, and requirement alignment.
Built monitoring, alerting, and observability pipelines that improved incident detection, MTTR, and production debugging.
Designed AI quality controls including retrieval evaluation, guardrail profiles, per-silo policy enforcement, and schema-validated tool-calling outputs.
Delivered enterprise integration patterns through MCP tools, scalable APIs, and legacy-system bridges across multiple roles.
leadership_safe: Mentor and design-review driver for senior engineers.
Cross-functional stakeholder translator on architecture, risk, and technical-debt tradeoffs.
Hiring-process contributor with interview and assessment design experience.
unsafe_or_too_weak: Head of AI, VP of AI, or Chief AI Officer.
Formal Engineering Manager with a sizeable reporting line.
Primary model scientist for custom LLM training, fine-tuning, or RLHF.
Broad EEA AI governance lead beyond documented GDPR/TCF and content-policy controls.
Dedicated ML platform owner of full end-to-end MLOps across multiple programs.

well_represented_now: Current summary already surfaces large-scale architecture modernization and delivery outcomes from the strongest Seven.One transformation story.
Project layer already surfaces meaningful AI platform depth through Commander-4 and Lantern, including retrieval, evaluation, semantic caching, and gateway design.
Skills representation is already richer on applied AI than the summary, with retrieval evaluation, LLM-as-judge, structured outputs, MCP tools, and gateway design present.
underrepresented_now: Architect/player-coach framing is weaker than the evidence base supports because the headline remains generic and the metadata does not yet surface upstream Technical Architect title evidence.
AI quality controls are underrepresented in the summary: evaluation, guardrails, structured outputs, and per-silo policy controls sit below the fold despite being category-differentiating.
Stakeholder translation, risk communication, and subject-matter influence are present in curated achievements but not prioritized in the current narrative.
Reliability proof exists, but the representation leans more on transformation than on ongoing operational excellence and observability leadership.
Enterprise integration is fragmented across projects and earlier roles instead of being presented as one continuous through-line into production AI.
overstated_risk_now: The generic headline 'Engineering Leader / Software Architect' can be read as broader formal management than the evidence clearly supports for this category.
The summary over-centers legacy modernization and understates current production-AI architecture, which can make the profile read more platform-modernization than AI-platform leadership.
Because the representation is proxy-derived, fit judgments on headline/summary balance remain provisional until a canonical master CV artifact exists.
representation_priorities: Make the headline architecture-forward and production-AI-specific without using executive AI titles.
Rebalance the summary so Commander-4 evaluation, guardrails, and platform-scale proof appear alongside legacy modernization.
Promote one stakeholder-translation bullet and one reliability/observability bullet into the top Seven.One narrative.
Cluster skills around architecture, observability, evaluation, guardrails, and integration before stack-specific tools.

curation_priorities.action: Promote claimable current-role title variants from the knowledge base into structured metadata so architect/player-coach framing becomes downstream-safe.
curation_priorities.why_now: Headline/title-family fit is one of the cleanest category gains available, and the evidence already appears claimable upstream.
curation_priorities.expected_category_impact: High: strengthens safe Architect / Technical Lead framing and reduces pressure to use noisy executive titles.
curation_priorities.citation: Upstream STAR records explicitly support 'Lead Software Engineer / Technical Architect' and 'Technical Lead - Addressable TV'.

curation_priorities.action: Curate richer structured provenance for current-role stakeholder and risk-communication evidence if the underlying STAR records contain more specific scope or outcomes than the current bullets expose.
curation_priorities.why_now: This market rewards stakeholder translation and player-coach influence; stronger provenance here would improve both curation completeness and representation quality.
curation_priorities.expected_category_impact: Medium-high: sharpens leadership-scope fit without drifting into formal management claims.
curation_priorities.citation: Tracker and index entries point to current-role stakeholder and risk items as relevant, but they are prioritization hints until backed by source-grade evidence.

curation_priorities.action: Promote Daypaio title and role-scope evidence into metadata to show a longer-running pattern of technical-lead/player-coach ownership before the current role.
curation_priorities.why_now: Continuity across roles improves credibility for architect/player-coach positioning and reduces overreliance on one current role.
curation_priorities.expected_category_impact: Medium: strengthens longitudinal leadership-scope fit and supports category-consistent role weighting.
curation_priorities.citation: The knowledge-base record for DAYPAIO uses 'Senior Software Engineer Full Stack - Tech Lead', which aligns with the category’s preferred non-executive leadership pattern.

master_cv_upgrade_actions.action: Replace the generic headline with an architect-forward, production-AI-specific line that emphasizes platform architecture, delivery, reliability, and evaluation rather than broad leadership alone.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.why_now: The current headline is safe but too generic, and the evidence supports a much tighter match to the category’s real demand pattern.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.citation: Architecture, production AI, and player-coach scope are already evidenced; the headline should reflect that directly.

master_cv_upgrade_actions.action: Rewrite the summary so it pairs the legacy modernization story with Commander-4 platform leadership, evaluation, guardrails, and observability instead of letting the AdTech transformation dominate alone.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.why_now: Summary balance is currently the biggest representation gap between existing evidence and category fit.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.citation: The current summary captures architecture delivery well but misses the strongest AI-quality-control evidence already in the record.

master_cv_upgrade_actions.action: Reorder top Seven.One bullets so the current-role story reads as architecture + production AI quality + stakeholder influence, not architecture alone.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.why_now: This category rewards a combined narrative of architecture, delivery, reliability, evaluation, governance, and translation.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.citation: Several category-critical bullets already exist but are not prioritized together in the top narrative.

master_cv_upgrade_actions.action: Regroup skills around platform architecture, observability, evaluation, guardrails, integration, and gateway design before lower-level tool names.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.why_now: The current skills layer is technically rich but can read tool-heavy rather than architecture-and-quality oriented.
master_cv_upgrade_actions.citation: Verified and supporting skills already cover enterprise AI platform, guardrail engineering, LLM-as-judge evaluation, MCP tool design, SLO/SLI design, and gateway design.
